content: nd rename/move all files accordingly, regardless of their original paths. The unified model should be general and extensible enough to accommodate new types, since old systems often had ad-hoc categories that might not directly align. By doing this harmonization, applications and searches in MythOS can treat all data uniformly without needing to know its origin  – the user of MythOS just sees one hierarchy or one graph. • Identifier Mapping (Crosswalks): In merging taxonomies or ID schemas, often you need a mapping table – essentially a cross-reference that says ID_A from System X corresponds to ID_7 in System MythOS, etc. Building these mappings can be labor-intensive but is crucial for thread resolution. One approach is to use existing unique information to connect items: for example, matching by file hash or by title. If two systems have overlapping content, computing a fingerprint (hash) of each file can identify duplicates despite different names, allowing you to collapse them into one. For naming collisions (different items with same name), a strategy is to concatenate context to the name or assign new surrogate keys. Imagine 10 folders each with a Notes.txt – in MythOS, you
